title: A Compact, Robust, and Tunable Open Microcavity Platform for Solid-State Quantum Electrodynamics
authors: Thi D. Hoang, Fateme Mahdikhany, Zixuan Wang, Richard Mirin, Kevin Silverman, Poolad Imany, Shuo Sun
abstract: Open microcavities provide a powerful platform for studying cavity quantum electrodynamics in solid-state systems. However, operating open microcavities at cryogenic temperatures, as required for many solid-state quantum emitters, typically demands bulky and cryostat-specific vibration-mitigation setups. Here we report a compact, robust, and tunable mechanical host for an open microcavity. The complete mechanical assembly fits within a footprint of $1'' \times 1'' \times 0.5''$. Using this mechanical host, we observe no vibration-induced cavity broadening for an open microcavity with finesse exceeding 1,000 without cryostat customization or active locking. The assembly also enables in situ tuning of the cavity resonance over 3 nm, and the resonance of the same cavity remains within this range across multiple cooldowns. To further showcase the capability of this assembly, we demonstrate coupling between an InGaAs quantum dot and an open microcavity with a cooperativity exceeding unity. This platform provides a versatile testbed for fundamental cavity quantum electrodynamics and a scalable route to portable quantum light sources and spin-photon interfaces for quantum repeaters, quantum networks, and photonic quantum computing.
summary: The researchers have created a tiny, vibration-resistant mechanical assembly for open microcavities that works reliably in standard cryostats. This platform allows for easy tuning of cavity resonance and has successfully demonstrated strong coupling with quantum dots. It offers a scalable path toward portable quantum light sources and quantum computing components.
main_problem: Operating open microcavities at cryogenic temperatures is challenging due to extreme sensitivity to mechanical vibrations and the need for bulky, custom vibration-mitigation setups.
main_result: The authors developed a compact, robust, and tunable mechanical host for an open microcavity that shows no vibration-induced broadening in a standard cryostat and achieves cooperativity exceeding unity with an InGaAs quantum dot.
method: The platform uses a piezo-driven assembly to tune cavity resonance in situ and employs cross-polarized transmission spectroscopy and photoluminescence to characterize the emitter-cavity coupling.
model_or_system: The system is a plano-concave Fabry-Perot resonator consisting of a GaAs/AlAs DBR bottom mirror and a microscale concave top mirror, coupled to InGaAs quantum dots embedded in a p-i-n junction.
key_observables: Cavity resonance, quantum-dot-induced transmission dips (dipole-induced transparency), cavity finesse, and cooperativity.
important_parameters: Finesse > 1,000, coupling strength g/2pi = 1.37 GHz, cooperativity C > 1, and a tuning range of 3 nm.
main_assumptions: The theoretical model for fitting spectra omits inhomogeneous spectral diffusion, and the model assumes the weak excitation limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the microcavity schematic and electric field profile; Figure 2 shows PL spectra and cavity modes; Figure 3 displays transmission spectra showing QD-induced dips, saturation, and Fano-like features; Supplemental figures show device yield and Stark tuning.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the vibration-mitigation challenge, describes the fabrication and architecture of the microcavity and mechanical host, presents characterization of cavity stability and tuning, demonstrates quantum dot coupling and nonlinear response, and concludes with scalability and device yield analysis.
why_interesting: This work provides a highly stable and scalable hardware platform for cavity QED, which is essential for implementing spin-photon interfaces and quantum networks in solid-state systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. (a) Schematic of the open microcavity used in our work, overlaid with the calculated electric field profile of its fundamental mode based on the cavity length estimated from experiment. The cavity is formed by a planar DBR with 29.5 pairs of alternating GaAs/AlAs layers and a microscale concave DBR with 12.5 pairs of alternating SiO2/SiN layers. A GaAs epitaxial layer with InGaAs quantum dots (yellow triangles, enlarged for visibility) embedded in a p-i-n diode (yellow solid lines, enlarged for visibility) is grown on top of the planar DBR. The lateral extent of the fabricated concave feature, marked by the white dashed lines, is approximately 6 𝜇m. (b) Design of the mechanical...

Fig. 2. Characterization of the open microcavity and its mechanical host. (a) Spectrum of a cavity with two adjacent longitudinal modes lying within the DBR bandgap, obtained via photoluminescence spectroscopy of the quantum dot ensemble. (b) Cross- polarized transmission spectrum of a different cavity at the center of the DBR bandgap, showing two non-degenerate polarization modes. (c) Transmission spectrum of the same cavity when the compressor of the cryostat is on (red triangles) and off (grey circles). The two spectra are nearly identical, showing that we have no vibration induced cavity broadening. (d) Photoluminescence spectrum of the cavity as we vary the bias voltage applied on the...

Fig. 3. Measurement of a cavity QED device with single quantum dots coupled to an open microcavity. (a) Cross-polarized cavity transmission spectrum of an open microcavity coupled with two distinct quantum dots. (b) Cross-polarized transmission spectrum of the 𝐻-polarized cavity mode (corresponding to the the dashed-line box region of (a)) as we increase the power of the incident laser. The power is measured right before the objective lens. (c) Cross-polarized transmission spectrum of the 𝐻-polarized cavity mode as we vary the detuning between the cavity and the quantum dot by varying the cavity length. In all panels, grey circles are measured data, and red solid lines are the theoretical...
